title: Staying Connected After a Move to Memory Care
summary: A move to memory care changes the shape of the relationship, but not its importance — here's how families stay meaningfully connected.
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Staying Connected After a Move to Memory Care | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: A move to memory care changes the shape of the relationship, but not its importance — here's how families stay meaningfully connected.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Shorter, more frequent visits often work better than long, infrequent ones.
Activities (music, old photos, simple tasks together) often connect better than conversation alone.
It's normal for visits to look different than they used to, and that's not a loss of the relationship itself.

paragraphs: Shorter visits, more often, tend to be less overwhelming and more successful than occasional long visits, particularly as a dementia progresses. Following your parent's energy and cues in the moment matters more than sticking to a planned visit length.

Shared activities — listening to familiar music, looking through old photos, folding laundry together — often create more genuine connection than conversation alone, especially as verbal communication becomes harder.

nextSteps: Try shorter, more frequent visits rather than occasional long ones.
Bring an activity, not just conversation, to visits.
Give yourself grace as the relationship's shape changes — connection can still be real, even when it looks different.
disclaimer: General guidance — the care team can offer suggestions specific to your parent's current stage and preferences.
